title: The Macro Minute: Is this the start of a correction or crash?

Happy Friday out there team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Friday, May
15th, 2026. As always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's lead
off morning note. So, let's dive right
in. Today's key macro question is, is
this the start of a correction or crash?
Our answer is a tolerable correction is
the highest probability outcome, meaning
investors
should sit tight and ride it out unless
our risk management overlays, KISS being
for retail, Dr. Mo being for
institutional, signal something
different in the coming days or weeks.
This correction may feel like a crash to
some because the most recent leg of the
42 macro paradigm seed bull market,
which began last year in April, was
driven by speculative fervor and signals
the presence of speculative excesses in
the AI trade. Traders who did not heed
our crowding models bearish signal in
the NASDAQ 100 from earlier this week
should take advantage of the elevated
price levels to take profits as the Fed,
ECB, Bank of England, and Bank of Japan
appear set to contribute to bond and
currency volatility in the coming
months. This increasingly probable
outcome will be bearish for global
equity and risk assets.
As always, we'll wrap up from a question
from our community here in our 42 macro
dashboard. This one's titled, is AI
creating some shadow liquidity? Says,
"Hi Darius, really appreciate all that
you do to keep us informed and safer and
sleeping better at night. Appreciate
you.
If you have time for a question, of
course I do. I wonder if you would
address the fact that some of the short
end of the rates complex is fairly close
to going into negative territory in
terms of real rates, and we know how the
large market participants borrow and
deploy because they get paid to take on
debt in real terms, and this typically
forces capital further out on the risk
curve. That is correct. But, will
negative rates have this effect moving
forward, or are there mitigating factors
this time around?" So, the first thing
I'll say is the assumption that real
rates, you know, stimulate borrowing is
very true. But, the thing I'll say, just
going to take it to my Bloomberg here,
you know, we're not we're not close to
real rates on the short end of the
curve. The blue line here being the
two-year real yield, the two-year
Treasury yield deflated by the two-year
inflation swap rate. We've been trading,
you know, basically around 100 basis
points on the real rate basis for
basically about a year now.
If you want to go find some real rates
or rates that are pretty close to real,
you got to go to China, you got to go to
the UK, you got to go to the Eurozone,
you got to go to Switzerland. That's
where you're going to be able to
finance,
you know, cheap leverage here. So, cheap
leverage
in capital markets. And that and by and
large that's what we're seeing, right?
We saw Google do the Japanese yen deal.
We're increasingly seeing AI access
global debt capital markets. I think to
the tune of about $300
billion over the past year or so
just to finance the AI CapEx. The number
one thing I would highlight as it
relates to
the growth of leverage, financial
leverage, is the continued decline in
the SOFR IRB spread, which in our
opinion is a function of the Fed's the
effectiveness of the Fed's reserve
management purchase program. They're
currently annualizing at about $640
billion
in terms of our RMP, and that's had a
pretty profound impact in returning the
repo market back to an
not necessarily abundant reserves
regime, but certainly something in that
direction. If you go look at the SOFR
IRB spread, we're now back at minus nine
basis points, minus four basis points on
a trailing three basis. These are both
the lowest levels we've seen
since the early fall of last year when
we began warning about the tightness in
the repo market and ultimately presaged
some of the volatility that we saw and
you know, from October really through
the end of March. And so, kind of
wrapping up, you know,
the the the Fed, you know, the the rest
of the the Fed and its peer central
banks are all moving in a hawkish
direction. We can see this in terms of
what's being priced in overnight index
swaps markets for the respective central
banks. Since the US-Israel-Iran conflict
began, the ECB
for example, rate hikes have been priced
into the ECB uh curve over the next
12-month basis. One rate hike has been
priced into uh the Bank of Japan curve
over the next 12-month basis with three
total uh I think it's four total uh for
the ECB. Uh two rate hikes have been
priced into the Swiss National Bank
curve with two rate hikes priced in
total. Uh five rate hikes have been
priced into the Bank of England curve
with three rate hikes priced total. And
then uh the Fed uh the the Fed curve the
US dollar curve is uh four rate hikes
have been priced in uh to to now you
know from from pricing in three rate
cuts to now pricing in one rate hike.
And so ultimately we expect the uh the
language uh coming from Kevin Warsh and
his colleagues on that FOMC uh at the to
be hawkish at the margins alongside the
rest of the global uh central banks uh
in the coming months. Uh target mid-June
for when you're really going to start to
see uh some movement uh by these central
banks in terms of what they're uh
pivoting their reaction functions in an
incrementally hawkish direction which
would ultimately exacerbate uh the bond
market volatility and the currency
market volatility that is starting to
brew but not yet really kicked off. And
the reason we think uh this could cause
problems in asset markets is because the
Fed is uh demonstrably lacking behind
its peer central banks in terms of what
it's priced in from a tightening
perspective only one rate hike versus
four for the ECB uh three uh for the
Bank of Japan three for the Bank of
England and two for the Swiss National
Bank. So if the Fed plays catch up there
you're going to have a stronger dollar
uh in the coming months and you're going
to have a decline in global liquidity
which obviously be very uh negative for
uh risk assets particularly uh the
crowded AI trade that is now starting to
uh come online unwound uh at the
margins. So we'll wrap it up there.
Darius Dale here presenting our uh macro
minute for Friday uh May 15th, 2026.
That's the luck out there today. We'll
catch back here on Monday. Have a great
weekend.
